senses.definition: A rare medical condition name sometimes used (or confused with) scleroderma, involving hardening/thickening of skin.
examples: The doctor checked whether the symptoms matched sclerodema or scleroderma.
Some older texts use the word sclerodema for skin-hardening illnesses.

senses.definition: A term used for the hard tissue of some corals’ skeleton, especially stony corals.
examples: Researchers described the coral’s scleroderm in the fossil record.
The scleroderm forms part of the coral skeleton that can persist after death.

senses.definition: A long-lasting autoimmune disease that can cause skin to harden and thicken and can also affect internal organs.
examples: She was diagnosed with scleroderma after her skin began to tighten.
Scleroderma can affect the lungs and kidneys in some patients.

senses.definition: A family name in biological classification (a “-aceae” family), related to organisms associated with the name scleroderm-.
examples: The student learned how sclerodermaceae fits into plant or fungal classification.
Taxonomists revised the boundaries of sclerodermaceae.

senses.definition: Plural of scleroderma (disease) or scleroderm (hard coral tissue), depending on context.
examples: In the clinic, doctors discuss different kinds of sclerodermas.
Textbooks may use “sclerodermas” when comparing multiple coral-tissue types.

senses.definition: A plural form used in technical writing, often referring to multiple occurrences or types related to “scleroderm-” (context determines whether it means scleroderma-like disease or scleroderm hard tissue).
examples: The paper listed several sclerodermata found in different specimens.
The author used sclerodermata in a technical, context-specific way.

senses.definition: A scientific order name in taxonomy (the “-ales” form), used for organisms classified under scleroderm-.
examples: The textbook grouped several genera under the order sclerodermatales.
A new study described features of sclerodermatales.

senses.definition: An inflammation condition name sometimes used in technical contexts that relates to hardening or thick skin (meaning depends on context and may be rare/variant usage).
examples: The dermatologist discussed whether the rash could be called sclerodermatitis.
Old medical notes used sclerodermatitis to describe skin inflammation with thickening.

senses.definition: Describing skin or tissue that is hard, thick, and tight, like in scleroderma-related conditions.
examples: The report described sclerodermatous changes in the skin.
Some people develop sclerodermatous areas that feel firm.

senses.definition: A technical plural form related to “scleroderm-”, referring to multiple instances/types; exact meaning depends on whether the text is about the disease or about biological tissue.
examples: The author compared several sclerodermi in different studies.
In that article, sclerodermi referred to a group of specimens.

senses.definition: A rare or variant word form used for scleroderma, referring to an autoimmune disease that can harden and thicken skin.
examples: Older literature used sclerodermia to describe the same condition as scleroderma.
Her doctor explained the term sclerodermia in relation to skin hardening.

senses.definition: Plural of sclerodermia (or sometimes used to mean multiple scleroderma cases/types, depending on context).
examples: The study compared sclerodermias across different patient groups.
Clinicians sometimes discuss sclerodermias as disease categories.

senses.definition: Related to scleroderma-like skin changes: hard, thick, and tight tissue.
examples: The exam showed sclerodermic skin texture changes.
Some patients have sclerodermic areas on the hands and face.

senses.definition: A term used in technical contexts for a piece/type of hard tissue associated with scleroderm- (often in coral or skeletal tissue discussions).
examples: The specimen included several sclerodermites.
The lab report described the structure of each sclerodermite.

senses.definition: Plural of sclerodermite: multiple hard tissue units/types described in technical work.
examples: Different sclerodermites were found in the same coral skeleton section.
The paper compared the size of sclerodermites.

senses.definition: Describing tissue or skin showing hardening/thickening characteristics related to scleroderm- (exact meaning depends on context).
examples: The clinician noted sclerodermitic characteristics in the skin.
The study used “sclerodermitic” to describe hardened tissue patterns.

senses.definition: Inflammation of skin or tissue described with a hardening/thickening association (often a rare or variant technical term).
examples: The notes mentioned sclerodermitis as part of the skin problem.
The doctor said the term sclerodermitis is used when inflammation affects thickened skin.

senses.definition: Having hard, thick, and tight characteristics of skin or tissue, similar to scleroderma-related changes.
examples: The physical exam described sclerodermous skin.
The disease can create sclerodermous areas that feel firm.

senses.definition: Plural form related to scleroderm-; in biology it may refer to hard tissue units, while in medicine it may be used informally/rarely for multiple scleroderma-like cases.
examples: The report compared scleroderms across samples.
In that chapter, scleroderms meant specific hard tissue types.

senses.definition: A technical term for something that forms or produces hardened material, often used in specialized science writing.
examples: The researchers discussed how the sclerogen process creates hardened tissue.
The term sclerogen is used in technical descriptions of tissue formation.

senses.definition: A technical plural form meaning multiple items related to “sclerogen-” (e.g., materials/processes that cause hardening), depending on the field.
examples: The paper listed several sclerogeni among its examples.
In that context, sclerogeni referred to hardening-related elements.

senses.definition: Causing or promoting hardening or formation of hard tissue/material.
examples: Certain factors are described as sclerogenic in tissue studies.
The lab report used the term sclerogenic to mean “promoting hard tissue.”

senses.definition: Resembling or relating to sclerogen-like hard tissue/material in specialized descriptions.
examples: The specimen showed sclerogenoid characteristics under the microscope.
The authors described the material as sclerogenoid.

senses.definition: Producing hard tissue/material, or relating to the production of hardening substances.
examples: The study described a sclerogenous pathway in tissue formation.
Some researchers use sclerogenous to describe processes that lead to hard tissue.

senses.definition: A biological cell type (often in plant tissue) that has thick walls and gives support.
examples: Scleroids help strengthen plant stems and leaves.
Botany students studied scleroids in the leaf tissue.
